title: Upgrade your widget to be compatible with Interface Version 2
breadcrumbs: Upgrade your widget to be compatible with Interface Version 2
html:
Upgrade your widget to be compatible with Interface Version 2

Add to My Topics
Add selected topic and subtopicsAdd Topic & Subtopics with topic headAdd entire publication
Watch
Download PDF
Save selected topicSave selected topic and subtopicsSave entire publication
Share
Email LinkCopy Link
Feedback
  • Last UpdatedMai 29, 2025
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

UX Widget Interface Version 2 introduces a new format for the data argument in the UX Widget Interface API functions: processLayout, onConnect, dataUpdate, and dataCleanup.

The key changes in the new data argument format include:

  • Different type of properties/data-internals: For example, properties such as html, style, class, value, and valrep are now contained in a single-level object, prefixed according to their category.
  • Removal of the 'uniface:' prefix: Properties previously prefixed with uniface: no longer include this prefix.

If you decide to upgrade your widget to support the version 2 interface without using the compatibility API, you need to:

  • Adapt how your widget handles objectDefinition and data parameters passed by the UX API’s in processLayout(), onConnect(), dataUpdate(), and dataCleanup().
  • Change the method implementation and logic to work with the new single-level argument structure.
  • Update function comments in your widget source file to align with new data format similar to templates\ux_widget.js. The template files are located in the following directory: uniface/webapps/ux_compatibility_utils/templates/.
  • Update the uxInterfaceVersion = 2 setting in the physical widget definition section of the web.ini file.
Table 1. Summary of Required Changes in Affected UX API Functions
API Function What Changed in Interface V2 Required Action

dataUpdate(data)

The data argument now contains all properties as individual key-value pairs, where each key includes the property category as a prefix (e.g., "data["html:disabled"]"), instead of being grouped under nested objects (e.g., data.html.disabled).

Update how data argument is used in the API. Access properties directly using string keys that include type prefixes ("html:", "style:", "class:")

dataCleanup(dataNames)

 The dataNames argument is now a Set of property names as strings prefixed by category (e.g., "dataNames.has("style:padding")"), instead of an object containing grouped Sets by category (e.g., dataNames.style.has("padding")).

Update how dataNamesargument is used in the API. Replace grouped access (dataNames.type.has(...)) with direct lookups in the Set using prefixed property names.

processLayout(objectDefinition)

 objectDefinition.getPropertyNames() function now returns all property names as a flat array using only known prefixes (html:, style:, class:). Uncategorized properties no longer have a uniface: prefix.

Handle properties without expecting a uniface: prefix; check for names directly.

onConnect(objectDefinition)

 Like processLayout(),objectDefinition .getPropertyNames() no longer includes a uniface: prefix for uncategorized properties.

Handle properties without expecting a uniface: prefix; check for names directly.

Example: dataUpdate(data) in Version 1

In Interface Version 1, the data argument was grouped by category into nested objects (e.g., html, style, class). Interface Version 2 replaces this with a flat structure using category-prefixed keys. To migrate to Version 2, developers must adapt their access logic accordingly.

Table 2. Structural Change – Shape of data Argument
V1 (Nested Object Format) V2 (Flat Object with Prefixed Keys)
html: { disabled: true } "html:disabled": true
style: { padding: "8px" } "style:padding": "8px"
class: { outline: true } "class:outline": true
value: "Test" "value": "Test"
valrep: [{ value: "1", representation: "One" }] "valrep": [{ value: "1", representation: "One" }]
Table 3. Access Pattern Change – How to Read Values in Your Code
V1 Access Expression V2 Access Expression
data.html.disabled data["html:disabled"]
data.style.padding data["style:padding"]
data.class.outline data["class:outline"]
data.value data["value"] or data.value
data.valrep data["valrep"] or data.valrep 
dataUpdate(data) { 
    // V1: data is grouped by category: data.html, data.style, data.class etc.
    // V1: Access grouped properties like data.html.disabled, data.style.padding
     if (data.html?.disabled) {
      this.inputElement.disabled = true;
    }
  
    if (data.style?.padding) {
      this.inputElement.style.padding = data.style.padding;
    }
  
    if (data.class?.outline) {
      this.inputElement.classList.add("outline");
    }
  
    if (data.value) {
      this.inputElement.value = data.value;
    }
  }

Example: dataUpdate(data) in Version 2

In Interface Version 2, the nested object structure was replaced by a flat key-value format. Each key is prefixed with its category name (e.g., html:title, style:color, class:name), eliminating nested groupings.

dataUpdate(data) {
   // V2: data has all properties on one level, prefixed with their category
   //V2: Access properties using flat keys like data["html:disabled"], data["style:padding"]
    if (data["html:disabled"]) {
      this.inputElement.disabled = true;
    }
  
    if (data["style:padding"]) {
      this.inputElement.style.padding = data["style:padding"];
    }
  
    if (data["class:outline"]) {
      this.inputElement.classList.add("outline");
    }
  
    if (data["value"]) {
      this.inputElement.value = data["value"];
    }
  }

Example: dataCleanup(dataNames) in Version 1

In Interface Version 1, the dataNames argument was structured as an object grouping properties by category (html, style, etc.) using Sets. In Version 2, this has changed to a single Set containing flat, prefixed property names.

Table 4. Structural Change – Shape of dataNames Argument
V1 (Grouped Set per type) V2 (Flat unified Set of strings)
{ html: Set("disabled") } Set("html:disabled")
{ style: Set("padding") } Set("style:padding")
{ class: Set("outline") } Set("class:outline")
{ uniface: Set("label-text", "clientsyntaxcheck") } Set("label-text", "clientsyntaxcheck")
Table 5. Access Pattern Change – How to Check Cleanup Targets
V1 Access Expression V2 Access Expression
dataNames.html.has("disabled") dataNames.has("html:disabled")
dataNames.style.has("padding") dataNames.has("style:padding")
dataNames.class.has("outline") dataNames.has("class:outline")

dataNames.uniface.has("label-

text")

 dataNames.has("label-text") 
dataCleanup(dataNames) {
  // V1: dataNames is an object of Sets grouped by type: dataNames.html, dataNames.style, etc.
  // V1: Access cleanup targets by checking in grouped Sets like dataNames.class.has("outline")
    if (dataNames.style?.has("padding")) {
      this.inputElement.style.padding = "";
    }
  
    if (dataNames.class?.has("outline")) {
      this.inputElement.classList.remove("outline");
    }
  }

Example: dataCleanup(dataNames) in Version 2

In Interface Version 2, the dataNames argument was simplified from a grouped object of Sets to a single flat Set containing category-prefixed property names (e.g., html.title, style.color), replacing the nested grouping used previously.

dataCleanup(dataNames) {
  // V2: dataNames is a flat Set of strings with prefixed keys.
  // V2: Access cleanup targets directly using dataNames.has("class:outline")
  if (dataNames.has("style:padding")) {
    this.inputElement.style.padding = "";
  }

  if (dataNames.has("class:outline")) {
    this.inputElement.classList.remove("outline");
  }
}

Example: getPropertyNames() in processLayout() / onConnect() in Version 1

In Interface Version 1, properties which are not prefixed with html, style, or class were returned with a uniface: prefix. Interface Version 2 removes this prefix, returning raw property names, simplifying direct access.

To migrate, developers must update checks that rely on uniface: prefixes and adjust calls to getProperty() to use unprefixed property names.

Table 6. Structural Change – Output from getPropertyNames()
V1 Output (Format of string in output array) V2 Output (Format of string in output array)
"html:disabled" "html:disabled"
"style:padding" "style:padding"
"uniface:label-text" "label-text"
"uniface:clientsyntaxcheck" "clientsyntaxcheck"

"uniface:changebutton" / "uniface:changebutton:icon"

 "changebutton" / "changebutton:icon"
Table 7. Access Pattern Change – How to Identify and Use Property Names
V1 Access Expression V2 Access Expression
names.includes("uniface:label-text") names.includes("label-text")
objectDefinition.getProperty("uniface:label-text") objectDefinition.getProperty("label-text")
names.includes("uniface:clientsyntaxcheck") names.includes("clientsyntaxcheck") 
processLayout(skeletonWidgetElement, objectDefinition) {
   // V1: getPropertyNames() returns an array with uniface: prefix for uncategorised properties.
// V1: Check property names like "uniface:label-text", "uniface:clientsyntaxcheck"
    const names = objectDefinition.getPropertyNames();
  
    if (names.includes("uniface:label-text")) {
      this.label.textContent = objectDefinition.getProperty("uniface:label-text");
    }
  }
  onConnect(widgetElement, objectDefinition) {
  // V1: Similar to processLayout, uses getPropertyNames() output with uniface: prefix.
  // V1: Check for property presence using names like "uniface:label-text"
    const names = objectDefinition.getPropertyNames();
  
    if (names.includes("uniface:label-text")) {
      this.label.textContent = objectDefinition.getProperty("uniface:label-text");
    }
  }

Example: getPropertyNames() in processLayout() / onConnect() in Version 2

Interface Version 2 removes the uniface: prefix, returning raw property names to simplify direct access.

processLayout(elem, objectDefinition) {
// V2: getPropertyNames() returns an array of flat property names without uniface: prefix.
// V2: Check directly using names like "label-text", "clientsyntaxcheck"
    const names = objectDefinition.getPropertyNames();
    
    if (names.includes("label-text")) {
      this.label.textContent = objectDefinition.getProperty("label-text");
    }
  }
  onConnect(widgetElement, objectDefinition) {
  // V2: Property names are returned directly without uniface: prefix.
  // V2: Check property presence using names like "label-text"    
    const names = objectDefinition.getPropertyNames();
  
    if (names.includes("label-text")) {
      this.label.textContent = objectDefinition.getProperty("label-text");
    }
  }

These changes make processing data arguments in complex widgets with sub-widgets significantly easier.

For more information about the data argument, see APIs: DSP UX Widget Class.

After upgrading your widget, update the uxInterfaceVersion = 2 setting in the physical widget definition section of the web.ini file. This indicates that the widget is implemented using Interface Version 2.

Was this topic helpful?
LikeDislike
